# The Gift of the Sky Weaver

Long ago, mountains stood bare and brown, reaching desperately toward the heavens. They envied the clouds that drifted freely above them, wearing brilliant white robes.

The youngest mountain, called Kael, cried out to the Sky Weaver, an ancient spirit who spun the clouds from starlight and mist. "Why must we remain dark and earthbound while the clouds dance in radiant beauty?"

The Sky Weaver descended, her fingers trailing silver threads. "You mountains are eternal guardians, holding up the very sky I weave. Yet you ask for beauty?"

"We ask only to wear a small piece of the heavens," Kael replied humbly.

Moved by their devotion, the Sky Weaver made a bargain. "Each winter, when the world grows cold and quiet, I shall drape you in blankets of white. But you must hold them carefully, for they are woven from clouds and dreams."

And so, as the first snow fell, the mountains received their crowns of white. To this day, they wear them proudly during winter's reign, standing as bridges between earth and sky. In summer, they release the melted snow as rivers, nourishing the grateful valleys below—the mountains' gift in return for their celestial adornment.

# The Salt Mill of the Sea

Long ago, the ocean was sweet as spring water. In those days, there lived two brothers—one generous, one greedy. The generous brother possessed a magical mill that could grind out anything its owner desired: gold, food, or precious jewels.

The greedy brother, consumed with envy, stole the mill one night. He loaded it onto his ship and sailed away, laughing at his cleverness. Once at sea, he commanded the mill, "Grind me salt to sell in every port!" The mill began to spin, pouring forth white crystals.

Salt filled the sacks. Then it covered the deck. Still the mill ground on.

"Stop!" cried the greedy brother, but he had never learned the stopping word. Salt piled higher as the ship rode lower in the water. He desperately pushed the mill overboard, but it was too late. The ship sank beneath the waves, dragged down by the weight of endless salt.

Deep on the ocean floor, the mill still grinds today, ceaselessly pouring out salt. This is why the seas are salty, and why the greedy find their treasures turn to bitterness. The generous brother still lives on land, content with enough, while his brother's greed seasons all the world's waters.

The elder sister used the lantern wisely, lighting the paths of lost travelers. The younger sister, jealous of how people praised her sibling, stole the lantern one evening. She climbed the highest mountain and proclaimed, "I shall keep this light forever! All will look only to me!"

As she raised the lantern high, it began to glow brighter and brighter, drawn by her selfishness. "Dim!" she cried, but the lantern knew no such command. The lantern shattered into countless pieces across the heavens, becoming the stars we see tonight. The younger sister transformed into the moon, forever reflecting stolen light but producing none of her own. The elder sister became the sun, willingly giving warmth and brightness to all below.

This is why the moon waxes and wanes—still seeking the wholeness she lost to greed, while the sun shines constant and generous upon the world.

Myth: Long ago, the forests stood in eternal silence, their branches reaching skyward but unable to speak. They watched birds sing and streams babble, envying those who could voice their joy.

The eldest oak, called Mora, called out to the Wind Dancer, an ancient spirit who traveled the world playing invisible music. "Why must we remain voiceless while all creation sings around us?"

The Wind Dancer paused her endless journey, her breath stirring the leaves. "You trees are patient witnesses, recording centuries in your rings. Yet you desire a voice?"

"We ask only to share in the world's chorus," Mora answered gently.

Touched by their longing, the Wind Dancer offered a gift. "I shall pass through your branches and you will sing. But your song will never be yours alone—it will always be our duet, wind and wood together."

The trees accepted gladly. From that moment, whenever the Wind Dancer traveled through the forest, the trees sang in rustling harmonies—soft whispers in gentle breezes, wild crescendos in storms.

To this day, trees cannot sing without the wind, and the wind finds its truest voice among the branches. Each needs the other to create the ancient song of the forest, a melody woven from partnership and patience.

Seeing this beauty, Day and Night finally understood. They agreed to share the sky, each taking turns, with Dawn and Dusk forever mediating their transitions. This is why twilight remains the most beautiful time—a reminder that cooperation creates wonders neither extreme could achieve alone, and that true power lies not in dominance but in balance.
